title: Towards sample-optimal learning of bosonic Gaussian quantum states
summary_bullets: This paper establishes nearly tight sample-complexity bounds for learning an n-mode bosonic Gaussian state (energy ≤ E) to ε trace distance: it proves lower bounds Ω(n^3/ε^2) for Gaussian/classical measurements and Ω(n^2/ε^2) for arbitrary quantum measurements, and matches these in several regimes with constructive protocols. It shows a ~O(n^2/ε^2) upper bound when the state is promised to be pure or passive (Gaussian measurements suffice for pure states but non-Gaussian measurements are provably required for optimal learning of passive states), and proves a nearly-tight ~Θ(E/ε^2) bound for non-adaptive single-mode Gaussian measurements that demonstrates adaptivity is essential to remove energy dependence. As key technical contributions, the authors derive sharp bounds relating trace distance to total-variation distance of Wigner functions and obtain a nearly-tight Θ̃(n^2/ε^2) sample complexity for learning Gaussian Wigner distributions, clarifying when classical phase-space sampling suffices versus when quantum (non-Gaussian or adaptive) resources are necessary.

Why Yanbei would care: Gaussian states describe the output light in interferometric sensing (e.g., gravitational-wave and dark-matter searches) and are central to optomechanics and quantum metrology topics in his prior work; the paper quantifies fundamental limits on how many experimental runs are required to characterize such states and shows when standard (heterodyne/homodyne) measurements are information-theoretically suboptimal, which directly impacts experimental design for low-noise, time-limited probes and benchmarking of macroscopic quantum systems. The results also intersect his current interests in quantum learning and quantum measurement theory (clarifying roles of adaptivity and non-Gaussian measurements) and bear on proposals that rely on precise state characterization to detect subtle effects such as gravity-induced decoherence or entanglement in tabletop optomechanical setups.

Keywords/topics: bosonic Gaussian states, quantum state tomography, sample complexity, Gaussian vs non-Gaussian measurements, Wigner function vs trace distance, adaptive measurements, optomechanics, quantum metrology, gravitational-wave/dark-matter sensing. Published on arXiv 2026-03-18 (v1, ~3.6 days ago).
